\textbf{18.3} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Action should be given up as an evil, say some thoughtful men; others say the acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity should not be given up.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.4} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Hear my conclusion on this relinquishment, O best of the Bharatas; for relinquishment, O tiger among men, is declared to be of three kinds.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.5} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Acts of sacrifice, gift, and austerity should not be given up but performed; for sacrifice, gift, and austerity are purifiers of the wise.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.6} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Yet even these actions should be done giving up attachment and their fruits; this, O Partha, is my settled and highest conviction.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.7} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But the giving up of an enjoined action is not fitting; to abandon it through delusion is held to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.8} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
He who gives up an action merely as troublesome, fearing bodily pain, performs a rajasic relinquishment and gains no fruit of it.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.9} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But when an enjoined action is done simply because it ought to be, O Arjuna, giving up attachment and fruit—that relinquishment is held sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.10} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The relinquisher filled with sattva, wise and free of doubt, neither shuns disagreeable action nor clings to agreeable action.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.11} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
For the embodied cannot give up actions entirely; but he who gives up the fruit of action is called a relinquisher.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.12} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
For those who do not relinquish, the fruit of action after death is threefold—unwanted, wanted, and mixed; but for those who renounce, there is none at all.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.13} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Learn from me, O mighty-armed one, these five factors, declared in the teaching of Sankhya for the accomplishment of all actions:
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.14} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
the seat of action, the agent, the various instruments, the many and diverse movements, and divine providence as the fifth.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.15} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Whatever action a person sets in motion with body, speech, or mind, right or wrong, these five are its factors.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.16} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
That being so, the one who, with untrained understanding, sees the self alone as the agent—that fool does not see truly.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.17} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
He who is free of the sense of 'I,' whose understanding is untainted, though he slay these people, does not slay, and is not bound.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.18} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Knowledge, the object of knowledge, and the knower—these are the threefold spur to action; the instrument, the act, and the agent—these are the threefold makeup of action.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.19} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Knowledge, action, and the agent are said, in the reckoning of the gunas, to be of three kinds, by the difference of the gunas; hear of these too, as they are.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.20} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The knowledge by which one sees in all beings a single, imperishable being, undivided amid the divided—know that knowledge to be sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.21} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But the knowledge that sees in all beings only manifold separate beings, each apart—know that knowledge to be rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.22} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But the knowledge that clings to one single thing as if it were the whole, without reason, missing the truth, and narrow—that is called tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.23} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Action that is enjoined, done without attachment, without craving or aversion, by one who seeks no fruit, is called sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.24} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But action done with great effort by one seeking to satisfy desire, or out of egotism, is called rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.25} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Action undertaken through delusion, heedless of its consequence, of loss and harm, and of one's own power, is called tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.26} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
An agent free of attachment and ego, possessed of resolve and zeal, unmoved in success and failure, is called sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.27} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
An agent driven by passion, craving the fruit of action, greedy, cruel, impure, swept by joy and grief, is called rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.28} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
An agent undisciplined, vulgar, stubborn, dishonest, malicious, slothful, despondent, and procrastinating is called tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.29} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Now hear, O Dhananjaya, the threefold division of understanding and of resolve, according to the gunas, set out fully and one by one.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.30} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The understanding that knows action and renunciation, what is to be done and not done, fear and fearlessness, bondage and liberation, O Partha, is sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.31} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The understanding by which one wrongly grasps dharma and adharma, and what is to be done and not done, O Partha, is rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.32} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The understanding that, wrapped in darkness, takes adharma to be dharma and sees all things inverted, O Partha, is tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.33} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The resolve by which one holds steady the workings of the mind, the breath, and the senses, through unwavering yoga—that resolve, O Partha, is sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.34} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But the resolve by which one clings, with attachment to fruit, to dharma, pleasure, and wealth, longing for their reward, O Partha, is rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.35} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The resolve by which the dull-witted will not let go of sleep, fear, grief, despair, and folly—that resolve, O Partha, is tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.36} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Now hear from me, O best of the Bharatas, the threefold happiness, that in which one comes to delight through practice and reaches the end of suffering.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.37} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The happiness that is like poison at first but like nectar in the end, born of the serene clarity of one's own understanding, is called sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.38} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The happiness born of the contact of the senses with their objects, like nectar at first but like poison in the end, is held to be rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.39} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The happiness that deludes the self both at first and in the end, arising from sleep, sloth, and heedlessness, is called tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.40} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
There is no being, on earth or among the gods in heaven, that is free of these three gunas born of prakriti.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.41} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The duties of brahmins, kshatriyas, vaishyas, and shudras, O scorcher of foes, are apportioned by the gunas that arise from their own natures.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.42} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Calm, restraint, austerity, purity, patience, and uprightness; knowledge, realization, and faith in the unseen—these are the duty of a brahmin, born of his nature.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.43} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Valor, vigor, fortitude, skill, not fleeing in battle, generosity, and lordly bearing—these are the duty of a kshatriya, born of his nature.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.44} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Farming, herding, and trade are the duty of a vaishya, born of his nature; and service is the duty of a shudra, born of his nature.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.45} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Devoted each to his own work, a person attains perfection; hear how one devoted to his own work finds it.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.46} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
He from whom all beings spring, and by whom all this is pervaded—worshiping him through his own work, a person attains perfection.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.47} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Better one's own dharma, imperfectly done, than another's dharma well performed; doing the work set by one's own nature, one incurs no fault.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.48} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
One should not give up the work born of one's nature, O son of Kunti, even if it is flawed; for all undertakings are wrapped in flaw, as fire in smoke.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.49} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
He whose understanding is unattached everywhere, his self subdued, his longing gone, attains through renunciation the supreme perfection of freedom from action.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.50} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Hear from me in brief, O son of Kunti, how one who has attained perfection attains Brahman as well—that highest consummation of knowledge.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.51} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Yoked with a purified understanding, restraining the self with firmness, turning away from sound and the other objects of sense, casting off craving and aversion,
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.52} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
dwelling apart, eating little, his speech, body, and mind controlled, ever absorbed in the yoga of meditation, taking refuge in dispassion,
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.53} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
freed from ego, force, pride, desire, anger, and possession, without a sense of 'mine,' and at peace—he is fit for the state of Brahman.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.54} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Become Brahman, serene in self, he neither grieves nor craves; the same toward all beings, he attains supreme devotion to me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.55} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
By devotion he comes to know me, who and how great I truly am; and knowing me in truth, he enters into me forthwith.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.56} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Ever performing all actions, taking refuge in me, by my grace he reaches the eternal, imperishable abode.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.57} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Surrendering all actions to me in your thought, intent on me, taking refuge in the yoga of understanding, keep your mind ever fixed on me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.58} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
With your mind on me, you will cross over all difficulties by my grace; but if through ego you will not listen, you will be lost.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.59} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
If, relying on your ego, you think, 'I will not fight,' vain is this resolve of yours; your own nature will compel you.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.60} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Bound by your own work, born of your nature, O son of Kunti, what through delusion you wish not to do, you will do even against your will.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.61} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The lord dwells in the heart of all beings, Arjuna, whirling them all about by his maya, as though mounted on a machine.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.62} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Take refuge in him alone with all your being, O Bharata; by his grace you will attain supreme peace and the eternal abode.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.63} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Thus I have declared to you the knowledge more secret than all secrets; ponder it fully, and then do as you wish.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.64} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Hear again my supreme word, the most secret of all; you are greatly loved by me, and so I shall tell you what is for your good.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.65} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Fix your mind on me, be devoted to me, sacrifice to me, bow before me; so you will come to me. I promise you truly, for you are dear to me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.66} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Giving up all dharmas, take refuge in me alone; I will free you from all sins—do not grieve.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.67} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
This is never to be told by you to one without austerity or devotion, to one who will not listen, or to one who scorns me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.68} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
He who shall declare this supreme secret among those devoted to me, offering me the highest devotion, will come to me beyond doubt.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.69} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Among men there is none who does me dearer service, and there will be no one on earth dearer to me than he.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.70} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
And he who studies this our dialogue on dharma—by him I shall be worshiped with the sacrifice of knowledge; so I hold.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.71} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
And the one who merely hears it, full of faith and free of scorn—he too, set free, will attain the happy worlds of those who do right.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.72} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Have you heard this, O Partha, with mind one-pointed? Has your delusion born of ignorance been destroyed, O Dhananjaya?
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.73} \textit{(Arjuna)}\par
My delusion is gone; by your grace, O Acyuta, I have regained my memory. I stand firm, my doubts dispelled; I will do your word.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.74} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
Thus have I heard this wondrous dialogue between Vasudeva and the great-souled Partha, making my hair stand on end.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.75} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
By the grace of Vyasa I have heard this supreme and most secret yoga directly from Krishna, the lord of yoga, as he himself declared it.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.76} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
O king, recalling again and again this wondrous and holy dialogue of Keshava and Arjuna, I rejoice again and again.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.77} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
And recalling again and again that most wondrous form of Hari, great is my wonder, O king, and I rejoice again and again.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.78} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
Wherever Krishna, the lord of yoga, is, and wherever Partha, the archer, is, there, I hold, are fortune, victory, well-being, and steadfast right conduct.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\clearpage
# Glossary

## Concepts

\begin{description}
\item[Atman] True Self, Soul, or inner principle; kept distinct from ordinary ego or individual personality. \textit{Forms: Atman | Ātman}
\item[bhakti] Devotion or devoted participation, especially toward the divine. \textit{Forms: bhakti | bhakti-yoga}
\item[Brahman] The ultimate reality or absolute principle. \textit{Forms: Brahman | Brahma-nirvana | Brahman-nirvana | Brahma-yoga | Brahma-sūtra | Brahman-realization}
\item[buddhi] Intellect, discernment, or decisive understanding. \textit{Forms: buddhi | buddhi-yoga}
\item[dharma] Order, duty, law, or right conduct; the exact sense depends on context. \textit{Forms: dharma | dharma-field}
\item[guna] A quality or constituent tendency of prakriti: sattva, rajas, and tamas. \textit{Forms: guna | gunas | guṇa | guṇas}
\item[karma] Action, deed, or the consequences bound to action. \textit{Forms: karma | karma-yoga}
\item[kshetra] Field, especially the body or field of experience in Chapter 13. \textit{Forms: kshetra | kṣetra}
\item[kshetrajna] Knower of the field; the conscious principle related to kshetra. \textit{Forms: kshetrajna | kṣetrajña}
\item[maya] Power of appearance, concealment, or manifestation; context-dependent. \textit{Forms: maya | Maya | māyā | Māyā | yoga-maya}
\item[nirvana] Extinction, peace, or release; here often linked with Brahman. \textit{Forms: nirvana | nirvāṇa | Brahma-nirvana | Brahman-nirvana}
\item[prakriti] Nature, material process, or the field of change and manifestation. \textit{Forms: prakriti | Prakriti | prakṛti | Prakṛti}
\item[purusha] Person, spirit, or conscious principle; meaning varies by passage. \textit{Forms: purusha | Purusha | puruṣa | Puruṣa | Purushottama}
\item[rajas] The guna associated with activity, passion, impulse, and restlessness. \textit{Forms: rajas | rājasika | Rajas}
\item[samsara] The ongoing cycle of worldly becoming, birth, death, and attachment. \textit{Forms: saṃsāra | samsara}
\item[Sankhya] A path or framework of discriminative knowledge; here not always a formal school label. \textit{Forms: Sankhya | Sāṅkhya | Sāṅkhyas | Sāṅkhya-yoga}
\item[sannyasa] Renunciation or giving up, especially of possessive attachment to action. \textit{Forms: sannyasa | sannyāsa}
\item[sattva] The guna associated with clarity, balance, and illumination. \textit{Forms: sattva | sāttvika | Sattva}
\item[tamas] The guna associated with inertia, dullness, confusion, and obstruction. \textit{Forms: tamas | tāmasika | Tamas}
\item[tyaga] Abandonment, renunciation, or relinquishing attachment to the fruits of action. \textit{Forms: tyāga | tyaga}
\item[yoga] Discipline, practice, integration, or path toward steadiness and liberation. Literal meaning is "yoking", "uniting", "joining" (from the root yuj: "to yoke", "to unite"). \textit{Forms: yoga | yogī | yogi | karma-yoga | buddhi-yoga | Sāṅkhya-yoga | bhakti-yoga | practice-yoga | yoga-maya}
\end{description}

## Social and ritual terms

\begin{description}
\item[acharya] Teacher or preceptor. \textit{Forms: ācārya | acharya}
\item[Brahmin] A member of the priestly or learned class; Sanskrit brāhmaṇa. \textit{Forms: Brahmin | brahmin | brāhmaṇa | brāhmaṇas}
\item[kshatriya] Member of the warrior or ruling class. \textit{Forms: kshatriya | kṣatriya}
\item[Om] Sacred syllable used as a concise sign of the absolute or divine. \textit{Forms: Om | AUM}
\item[shudra] Member of the service class in the traditional varna scheme. \textit{Forms: śūdra | śūdras | shudra | shudras}
\item[vaishya] Member of the class associated with trade, agriculture, and herding. \textit{Forms: vaiśya | vaiśyas | vaishya | vaishyas}
\item[Vedas] Ancient sacred texts of the Vedic tradition. \textit{Forms: Veda | Vedas | Ṛg | Sāma | Bṛhat-sāma | Gāyatrī}
\item[yajna] Sacrifice, ritual offering, or consecrated action. \textit{Forms: yajna | yajña | adhiyajña}
\end{description}

## Names and epithets

\begin{description}
\item[Acyuta] Epithet of Krishna, often glossed as the unfailing one. \textit{Forms: Acyuta}
\item[Arjuna] Arjuna, the warrior addressed by Krishna. \textit{Forms: Arjuna}
\item[Bharata] A dynastic or ancestral name, often used as an address. \textit{Forms: Bharata | Bhārata | Bharatas | Bhāratas}
\item[Dhananjaya] Epithet of Arjuna, often understood as winner of wealth. \textit{Forms: Dhananjaya | Dhanañjaya}
\item[Govinda] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Govinda}
\item[Gudakesha] Epithet of Arjuna, commonly linked with mastery over sleep. \textit{Forms: Guḍākeśa | Gudakesha}
\item[Hrishikesha] Epithet of Krishna, often understood as lord of the senses. \textit{Forms: Hṛṣīkeśa | Hrishikesha}
\item[Janardana] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Janardana | Janārdana}
\item[Kaunteya] Epithet of Arjuna: son of Kunti. \textit{Forms: Kaunteya | Kunti}
\item[Keshava] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Keshava | Keśava}
\item[Krishna] Krishna, speaker of the teaching to Arjuna. \textit{Forms: Krishna | Kṛṣṇa}
\item[Madhava] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Madhava | Mādhava}
\item[Madhusudana] Epithet of Krishna: slayer of Madhu. \textit{Forms: Madhusudana | Madhusūdana}
\item[Pandava] One of the sons or descendants of Pandu; often refers to Arjuna and his brothers. \textit{Forms: Pāṇḍava | Pāṇḍavas | Pandava | Pandavas | Pāṇḍu}
\item[Partha] Epithet of Arjuna: son of Pritha/Kunti. \textit{Forms: Partha | Pārtha | Pṛthā}
\item[Vasudeva] Name or epithet of Krishna, son of Vasudeva. \textit{Forms: Vāsudeva | Vasudeva}
\end{description}
